# BoardGame — YellowHapax

> A two-player pass-and-play abstract strategy game running in the browser. A 5×5 grid. Three piece types. Two ways to win. No luck. The entire game fits in one screen; two people sit down at the same machine and play.

## Live

https://yellowhapax.github.io/BoardGame/

## What It Is

Two players take turns placing and moving pieces on a 5×5 board. Player 0 and Player 1 share one keyboard and screen. Each player starts with 21 stones and 1 capstone.

The goal is to build a road — an unbroken orthogonal chain of your pieces connecting any two opposite edges of the board.

## The Three Piece Types

**Flat** — Lies horizontal. Counts as road-building material. Can be stacked on. The default placement choice.

**Wall** — Stands vertical. Blocks roads (yours and theirs). Cannot be moved through normally. Counts as neither player's road. Useful for cutting off opponent paths.

**Cap (Capstone)** — A dome. Each player has exactly one. Builds roads. Can flatten a standing Wall by moving onto it alone — the only piece with this ability. Cannot be stacked over; it terminates any stack it tops.

## Two Ways to Win

**Road Win** — Connect your two opposite edges (top↔bottom or left↔right) with an unbroken orthogonal chain of your flats and/or caps. Diagonals do not count. Walls do not count. This is checked immediately when any piece is placed or a move completes.

**Flat Win** — When every square on the board is occupied (no empty cells), count how many top-of-stack flats each player controls. Most flats wins. Walls and caps are ignored for this count. Tie is possible.

A road win for the moving player is checked before the board-full condition.

## Stack Movement

Any stack you own (top piece is yours) can be picked up. You carry up to 5 pieces from the top of the stack. You then move in one cardinal direction, dropping at least one piece on each square you pass through. You cannot change direction mid-move. You cannot move through a cap. You cannot move through a wall unless your carried piece is a lone cap, which flattens the wall on contact.

## Game Flow (Phases)

```
idle → [click own stack] → pickup
idle → [click piece type in inventory] → place → [click empty square] → idle (turn passes)
pickup → [choose carry count] → move → [click adjacent squares to drop] → idle (turn passes)
```

Cancelling a pickup returns all carried pieces to their origin square. Cancelling during place mode just deselects the piece type.

## Implementation

- React 19 + Vite 6, TypeScript
- 3D rendering: Three.js via @react-three/fiber and @react-three/drei
- State: Zustand store (`src/store.ts`) — single source of truth for board, inventory, phase, carried pieces, and win state
- UI: Tailwind CSS v4, Lucide icons, overlaid HTML on top of the Canvas
- No server. No backend. No accounts. Entirely client-side.

## Source Layout

```
src/
  store.ts          — All game logic: placement, movement, road-win BFS, flat-win count
  App.tsx           — Root: GameCanvas + UI overlay
  components/
    GameCanvas.tsx  — Three.js Canvas, lighting, OrbitControls
    Board3D.tsx     — 5×5 grid, tile geometry, brass edges, book-corner brackets
    Piece3D.tsx     — Flat (extruded trapezoid), Wall (vertical trapezoid), Cap (hemisphere)
    UI.tsx          — Inventory buttons, palette picker, win screen, cancel/reset controls
```

## Verified Mechanics (Test Suite)

Road win:
- Empty board does not win
- Straight N-S line of flats wins
- A wall in the path blocks the road
- A cap in the path counts as road
- Snake E-W path wins (orthogonal connectivity, not straight-line)
- Opponent piece in path blocks
- A cap on top of a mixed stack counts
- Diagonal adjacency does not count

Stack movement:
- Carry count is correct
- Pieces drop in correct order across squares
- Phase returns to idle and turn passes after final drop
- Rejections: moving off-axis, moving through a cap, moving through a wall without a lone cap
- Wall flattening: lone cap flattens a wall; any other carried piece is rejected
- Cap termination: cap always lands on top and cannot be moved over

## Palettes

12 named color pairs selectable in-game. Both players' pieces are always rendered in the active palette — Player 0 gets the first color, Player 1 the second.

Default: Flame & Tide — orange (#ff6600) vs blue (#0099ff).
